# Transcrição: How_To_Stop_Going_BLANK_In_Important_Conversations

## [00:00 - 01:36]

Whenever I'm doing things like presentation and meetings, as soon as I get asked a question, I go blank. The panic starts setting in. I might know the answer, but it just won't come. Before I share some thoughts, tell me more about why you think it happens. If you can sometimes be, even though I might know the answer, then I doubt myself. What else? Just the confidence, especially when you've got other people around you which they know what they're talking about. Two really interesting things, but you see what happened there, right? Is the surface thing you thought was that, oh, I just go blank. But then the second layer was, I'm scared of what the people around me think because I believe they know the answers. So fear of judgment as well. It's not just going blank, there's fear of judgment there. To address that, there's two other things. It's really about addressing your level of confidence. Then it's also about really not caring too much about what other people think. And then that's what creates the outcome you're after. I can tell you one thing, and it's that if you wanna develop confidence, you have to make the unfamiliar more familiar. The unfamiliarity that you have is speaking to people who have more authority than you. So I think it's about engaging in dialogue with people who have more authority than you more often. Because as you do that more often, you understand them more, you have more rapport with them, you're more familiar with talking with them, that reduces some of that anxiety. So again, as we become more familiar with something, it becomes less scary. And then there's another thing, fear of judgment. There's one quote that helps me with this. And it's a quote from Mark Twain who says, you worry less about what people think about you when you realize how seldom they do. We fear so much judgment from everybody that's around us, but often people are not thinking about you, they're just thinking about themselves.
